test_text.title: Buying a New Car and Trade-in Details

audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear a conversation between a customer and a salesman at a car showroom.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example which has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Please take a seat.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, I'm thinking of buying a new car, and I'd like some advice.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sure, yes.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Had you got any particular make in mind?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm interested in a Lida.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I've had one before and liked it, but I haven't really made up my mind.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The customer states that she is interested in a Lide, so Lida has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Please take a seat.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, I'm thinking of buying a new car, and I'd like some advice.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sure, yes.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Had you got any particular make in mind?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm interested in a Lida.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I've had one before and liked it, but I haven't really made up my mind.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sure, we've got various models.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Um. Right, what about the engine size?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Any ideas?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. The one I've got at the moment's 1.2 liter engine, but I find it a bit slow on long journeys.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'd like a bit more power this time.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: A 1.4 should do.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I don't think I need a 1.6 or anything.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, I think the model you're looking at is the Max.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Here's a picture.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Have you got one in?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I'll take you to have a look at it in a minute.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'll just get a few more details.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Is there anything else to do with the engine?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What kind of gear change do you want?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I presume you want a manual.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'd want automatic.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I've never driven a car with manual gears.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well now, here's the color chart for the Max.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Have you given that any thought?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: This blue is very popular at the moment.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it is nice.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I like blue.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Um. What's it called?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Royal?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm, but actually I think I prefer this lighter shade here, sky.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, that's popular too.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I think I'll go for that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: You might have to wait a week or so for that color, but I assume that'll be OK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, fine.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well we can go outside and you can have a good look at one, and perhaps take it out.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. But first, can I just ask you about finance?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The cash price is going to be somewhere in the region of seven and a half thousand.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: How would you like to pay?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Are you in a position to pay cash, or would you need credit?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'd like credit provided the terms are reasonable.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, you can discuss that with my colleague in a moment.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We have various arrangements, and would you be interested in us taking your present car as part exchange?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, fine.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So I'll just need some details from you, and then we can do a valuation...
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Is that OK?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Fine, yes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Could I have your full name?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Wendy Harries, that's HARRIES.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And is that Mrs... Miss... Ms...?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's Doctor, actually.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And your address?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: 20 Green Banks.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Is that 'Green' spelled as in the color?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Alton.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Is that OLTON?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Not quite, it begins with an A, not an O.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, that's in Hampshire, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And do you know your post code?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Yes, it's GU89EW.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Do you have a daytime phone number?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, I work at the hospital, but it's a bit difficult to get hold of me.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I can give you a number just for messages, and then I'll get back to you when I can.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Is that OK?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's fine.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And about the car you have now, what make is it?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's a Conti.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Do you know the year or the model name?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I think it's 1996 and it's called a lion, like the animal.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Then it must be 1994 because they brought out the fox after that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, right, yes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Mileage? Roughly?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm not sure, I know it's less than 70,000.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What color is it?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's grey, metallic grey.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And one last thing, what sort of condition would you say it's in?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'd probably describe it as reasonable.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Do you need to see it?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's parked outside.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Not at the moment, no.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Perhaps you could call in one day next week.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER

test_text.title: Rexford University Overview and Key Information for Prospective Students

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear an admissions officer from a UK university talking to a group of postgraduate students in a university abroad,
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: about applying for a place at his university.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: As I said earlier, there is I think at Rexford an excellent combination of physical and geographical advantages, as well as having a rural setting and still being close to central London.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Something that will certainly be of interest to you is that Rexford is just 35 minutes from London airport.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: At Rexford we have a strong research capability.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We came seventh out of 101 universities in last year's research assessment, carried out by a government body, and did particularly well in your particular subjects.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Engineering and science.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Actually, we got a top research grade of 5 for engineering, geography and computer sciences.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: One further point, and I know from talking to you individually that a number of you may be looking for some experience in industry after the course.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Is that all our science and engineering research departments have unusually close relationships with industry in the area.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Anyway, that's enough sales talk from me.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'll just take a sip of this coffee that's just arrived.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And then I'll say something about what actually happens when you apply.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right, now, if you do decide to make an application, what you do is send it directly to me in my department.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I will then immediately send confirmation.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And the application process begins, and I'd like to say at this point that you shouldn't worry if this process doesn't work all that quickly.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I mean, occasionally there are postal problems, but most often the hold up is caused by references.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The people you give as referees, uh, shall we say, take their time to reply.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Anyway, it's absolutely normal for this process to take three to four months.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What I do in this period is keep in touch with you and reassure you that things are moving along.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: One of the ways we've devised to help you decide about applying, as well as later when you've been accepted.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hopefully, is to put you in contact with, if possible, a student from your own country who is at present studying with us.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What you can do is phone them up.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We will, of course, liaise between you - and discuss your concerns with them.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That way you can get an objective opinion of what you can expect if you come to live and study at Rexford.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Not only the academic atmosphere, but important details like what the leisure facilities are like, and whether the English weather and food are really as awful as everybody says.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: If you decide you can face it, the contact can also help you just before you leave, with tips on what to pack and that sort of thing.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: At the moment, I think we've got two second-year students and one postgraduate from this country.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now to move on to the other concerns you expressed earlier, at a UK university, as I'm sure you know, you will be in an environment where independent learning is the norm.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Which takes most students a while to adjust to, and at a time when you will be separated from your normal surroundings, and in most cases your family.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: This can be a difficult time, but remember that something like 25% of our student body are international students like yourselves, and that there are several organizations in the university and city, whose main purpose is to offer help.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And ensure that your time with us is enjoyable and useful.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: One or two of you touched on the subject of accommodation earlier, so I'll just add a few points.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It is the University's policy to give priority in the allocation of residence places to three categories.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And those are visiting students, exchange students, and new postgraduate students.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: However, demand exceeds supply, so there is still a need to put your name down early for campus accommodation.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Particularly if your family is accompanying you.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: This means that the earlier you decide whether you want to study with us, and so get the procedure moving.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The better it will be for everybody.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Um. Yes?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. What if you would prefer to live outside the university?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: If you're planning to live off campus, you've got to sort things out even earlier.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: As with everything in short supply, the good accommodation gets snapped up months before the beginning of term.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: In other words, if you're starting in October, you need to be thinking about it in June, or at the very latest July.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So you do need to think very carefully about what you need, how much you can afford to pay, well in advance.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What you can't do is leave it until a few days before the start of term.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The agencies in town are pretty good, it's just a matter of contacting them in good time.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Of course we have a full-time accommodation officer available to help all students.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: She'll get in touch with you when you're accepted.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: She's got plenty of contacts in the town, and will deal with the agencies on your behalf.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: One or two of you asked me earlier about your level of spoken English.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Obviously most of you have already achieved a lot.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I wish I could speak your language half as well.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Having said that though, I'm afraid the lecturers will make little or no allowance for the presence of non-native speakers in the audience.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So anything you can do to improve your spoken English.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Even beyond the pretty high levels most of you have already reached, will help make your stay with us that bit more fun for you.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Some extra practice before you arrive is worth more than for example, private lessons afterwards when you won't really have time.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, and one last thing before I invite further questions, it's very important that you always...
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 2, you will hear an admissions officer from a UK university talking to a group of postgraduate students in a university abroad,
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: about applying for a place at his university.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 15.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: As I said earlier, there is I think at Rexford an excellent combination of physical and geographical advantages, as well as having a rural setting and still being close to central London.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Something that will certainly be of interest to you is that Rexford is just 35 minutes from London airport.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: At Rexford we have a strong research capability.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: We came seventh out of 101 universities in last year's research assessment, carried out by a government body, and did particularly well in your particular subjects.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Engineering and science.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Actually, we got a top research grade of 5 for engineering, geography and computer sciences.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: One further point, and I know from talking to you individually that a number of you may be looking for some experience in industry after the course.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Is that all our science and engineering research departments have unusually close relationships with industry in the area.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Anyway, that's enough sales talk from me.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: I'll just take a sip of this coffee that's just arrived.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Thank you.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: And then I'll say something about what actually happens when you apply.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Right, now, if you do decide to make an application, what you do is send it directly to me in my department.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: I will then immediately send confirmation.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: And the application process begins, and I'd like to say at this point that you shouldn't worry if this process doesn't work all that quickly.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: I mean, occasionally there are postal problems, but most often the hold up is caused by references.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: The people you give as referees, uh, shall we say, take their time to reply.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Anyway, it's absolutely normal for this process to take three to four months.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: What I do in this period is keep in touch with you and reassure you that things are moving along.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: One of the ways we've devised to help you decide about applying, as well as later when you've been accepted.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Hopefully, is to put you in contact with, if possible, a student from your own country who is at present studying with us.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: What you can do is phone them up.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: We will, of course, liaise between you - and discuss your concerns with them.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: That way you can get an objective opinion of what you can expect if you come to live and study at Rexford.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Not only the academic atmosphere, but important details like what the leisure facilities are like, and whether the English weather and food are really as awful as everybody says.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: If you decide you can face it, the contact can also help you just before you leave, with tips on what to pack and that sort of thing.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: At the moment, I think we've got two second-year students and one postgraduate from this country.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now to move on to the other concerns you expressed earlier, at a UK university, as I'm sure you know, you will be in an environment where independent learning is the norm.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Which takes most students a while to adjust to, and at a time when you will be separated from your normal surroundings, and in most cases your family.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: This can be a difficult time, but remember that something like 25% of our student body are international students like yourselves, and that there are several organizations in the university and city, whose main purpose is to offer help.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: And ensure that your time with us is enjoyable and useful.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: One or two of you touched on the subject of accommodation earlier, so I'll just add a few points.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: It is the University's policy to give priority in the allocation of residence places to three categories.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: And those are visiting students, exchange students, and new postgraduate students.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: However, demand exceeds supply, so there is still a need to put your name down early for campus accommodation.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Particularly if your family is accompanying you.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: This means that the earlier you decide whether you want to study with us, and so get the procedure moving.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: The better it will be for everybody.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Um. Yes?
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Uh. What if you would prefer to live outside the university?
utterances.speaker: WOMAN

utterances.text: If you're planning to live off campus, you've got to sort things out even earlier.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: As with everything in short supply, the good accommodation gets snapped up months before the beginning of term.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: In other words, if you're starting in October, you need to be thinking about it in June, or at the very latest July.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: So you do need to think very carefully about what you need, how much you can afford to pay, well in advance.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: What you can't do is leave it until a few days before the start of term.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: The agencies in town are pretty good, it's just a matter of contacting them in good time.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Of course we have a full-time accommodation officer available to help all students.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: She'll get in touch with you when you're accepted.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: She's got plenty of contacts in the town, and will deal with the agencies on your behalf.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: One or two of you asked me earlier about your level of spoken English.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Obviously most of you have already achieved a lot.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: I wish I could speak your language half as well.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Having said that though, I'm afraid the lecturers will make little or no allowance for the presence of non-native speakers in the audience.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: So anything you can do to improve your spoken English.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Even beyond the pretty high levels most of you have already reached, will help make your stay with us that bit more fun for you.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Some extra practice before you arrive is worth more than for example, private lessons afterwards when you won't really have time.
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: Oh, and one last thing before I invite further questions, it's very important that you always...
utterances.speaker: MAN

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: What TWO advantages does the speaker say Rexford University has for the students he is speaking to?
options: higher than average results in examinations
good transport links with central London
near London Airport
special government funding
good links with local industry

items: When application is received, confirmation will be sent

items.prefix: Application processing may be slowed down by
University tries to put international applicants in touch with a student from the same <input> who can give information and advice on

items.title: The speaker says international students at UK universities will be
options: offered accommodation with local families.
given special help by their lecturers.
expected to work independently.

items.title: What does the speaker say about university accommodation on campus?
options: Most places are given to undergraduates.
No places are available for postgraduates with families.
A limited number of places are available for new postgraduates.

items.title: Students wishing to live off-campus should apply
options: several months in advance.
two or three weeks in advance.
at the beginning of term.

items.title: The university accommodation officer will
options: send a list of agents for students to contact.
contact accommodation agencies for students.
ensure that students have suitable accommodation.

items.title: With regard to their English, the speaker advises the students to
options: tell their lecturers if they have problems understanding.
have private English lessons when they arrive.
practise their spoken English before they arrive.

test_text.title: Annie and Ben's Discussion on Course Feedback Form

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear two friends discussing a course they have just done.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 27.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 27.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Oh, Ben, I just remembered, I never filled in that form for Nick.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Ah.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Did you do it?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: The course feedback form?
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Yes, if you want, we can do it together.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: I've got mine here.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Is that OK?
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: OK, let's have a look then.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: What do we have to do?
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Let's fill in the top first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Let's see, Course, Course code...
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Uh, it's communication in business.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Communication in business, I do know that, but what's the code?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: C B 1 6 something, C B 1 6 2, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Hmm. That's it.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: OK, and dates.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: When did we start?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: I remember my birthday's on May 4th, and it was the day after, it must have been May 5th.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Gosh, doesn't seem that long ago, does it?
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: No.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: And we finish at the end of this week on Friday, so that's July 15th?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Er, 16th, Monday was the 12th. Right, that was the easy bit.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Now let's have a look.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: "Please give your comments on the following aspects of the course".
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: OK, what's the first one?
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Oh, course organization.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: What do you think?
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Uh. Clear, it was, wasn't it?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, I think the organization was clear.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: OK, anything else for course organization?
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Um. It was a good thing he gave us the course outline at the beginning in the first session.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: That was useful, so I'll put that down, shall I?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Now, going on to suggestions for improvement, one thing that wasn't so good, I think we could have done a bit more work at the beginning.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: I mean at the beginning, it seemed dead easy.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: I thought it was gonna be really easy, and then all of a sudden, in the second half of the course, we got a whole load of work, reading to do and essays and things.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, it'd be better if it was more even.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: OK, now course delivery.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Does that mean teaching?
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Yeah, I suppose so.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Well, what I thought was really good on this course was the standard of teaching.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Actually, I mean some of the teachers were better than others, yeah, but the standard generally was fine, much better than other courses I've been on.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, I agree.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Let's put that then.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: OK, what about suggestions for improvement?
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: I didn't think it was all that wonderful when we had great long group discussion sessions that went on for hours and hours.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: I don't mean we shouldn't have group discussions, just that they shouldn't go on too long.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 28 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 28 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now on to materials and equipment.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Oh, now what was good about some sessions was the handouts.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Yes, I thought all the handouts were good, actually, and some were great with website addresses and everything.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Hmm. One problem though with materials was the key texts.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Yes. There just weren't enough copies on reserve in the library.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: And if you can't get the key text before the session, how are you supposed to do the reading?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, and not enough computers, you have to wait ages to get one.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: OK, testing and evaluation.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Well, I don't know.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: It's hard to say until we got our written assignments back.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Oh, don't talk about it.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: I only got mine in yesterday.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: It was a real struggle.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Oh, I hate to think what mark I'll get.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, but at least we've done the oral presentation.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Hmm. I thought that was good, the way I got my feedback really quickly.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Yes, it was.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: And I like the way we knew what it would be evaluated on.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: We knew the criteria, so we knew we had to think about clarity, organization, and so on.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, but I'm not so sure about the written work.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: One thing I think is that there's just too much.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: It's really stressful.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, I'd agree.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: And I don't see why they can't let us know the criteria they use for marking.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: The written assignments? But he told us.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: No, for the final exams.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Oh. What are they looking for?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: What are the criteria?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: What makes a pass or a fail?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, I never thought of that.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: It would be really useful.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Hmm. OK, any other comments?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: I thought student support was excellent.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Yeah, me too.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Excellent.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Other comments?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: No, I can't think of anything else.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Hmm, nor me.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: OK, so that's done.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Thanks, Ben.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: No.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: BEN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now on to materials and equipment.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Oh, now what was good about some sessions was the handouts.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: Yes, I thought all the handouts were good, actually, and some were great with website addresses and everything.

utterances.text: Hmm. One problem though with materials was the key texts.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: Yes. There just weren't enough copies on reserve in the library.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: And if you can't get the key text before the session, how are you supposed to do the reading?
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Yeah, and not enough computers, you have to wait ages to get one.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: OK, testing and evaluation.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: Well, I don't know.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: It's hard to say until we got our written assignments back.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: Oh, don't talk about it.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: I only got mine in yesterday.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: It was a real struggle.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Oh, I hate to think what mark I'll get.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Yeah, but at least we've done the oral presentation.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: Hmm. I thought that was good, the way I got my feedback really quickly.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: Yes, it was.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: And I like the way we knew what it would be evaluated on.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: We knew the criteria, so we knew we had to think about clarity, organization, and so on.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Yeah, but I'm not so sure about the written work.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: Hmm.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: One thing I think is that there's just too much.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: It's really stressful.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: Oh, yes, I'd agree.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: And I don't see why they can't let us know the criteria they use for marking.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: The written assignments? But he told us.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: No, for the final exams.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Oh. What are they looking for?
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: What are the criteria?
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: What makes a pass or a fail?
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Yeah, I never thought of that.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: It would be really useful.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: Hmm. OK, any other comments?
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: I thought student support was excellent.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: Yeah, me too.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: OK.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Excellent.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Other comments?
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: No, I can't think of anything else.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: Hmm, nor me.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: OK, so that's done.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Thanks, Ben.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: No.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: Thank you.
utterances.speaker: BEN

utterances.text: That is the end of section 3.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER

items: Please give your comments on the following aspects of the course:
useful to have <input> at beginning of course
too much work in <input> of the course - could be more evenly balanced
some <input> sessions went on too long
not enough copies of key texts available
marking criteria for oral presentations known
can we know criteria for marking final exams?

body.title: Feedback Form

answer_key.correct_answer: 5th May | 5 May | 5th of May | May 5th | May the 5th
16th July | 16 July | 16th of July | July 16th | July the 16th
outline | course outline | an outline | a course outline | the outline | the course outline
2nd half | the 2nd half | second half | the second half
teaching | teachers | standard of teaching | standard of teachers

test_text.title: Recycling of Household Waste in the UK: Challenges and Progress

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a student giving a presentation about a project on household waste recycling.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Well, my group has been doing a project on how household waste is recycled in Britain.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Uh. We were quite shocked to discover that only 9% of people here in the UK make an effort to recycle their household waste.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: This is a lower figure than in most other European countries, and needs to increase dramatically in the next few years, if the government is going to meet its recycling targets.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: The agreed targets for the UK mean that by 2008 we must reduce our carbon dioxide emissions by 12.5% compared with 1990.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: And recycling can help to achieve that goal in two main ways.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: The production of recycled glass and paper uses much less energy than producing them from virgin materials.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: And also recycling reduces greenhouse gas emissions from landfill sites and incineration plants.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: As part of our project, we carried out a survey of people in the street, and the thing that came up over and over again is that people don't think it's easy enough to recycle their waste.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: One problem is that there aren't enough drop off sites.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: That is the places where the public are supposed to take their waste.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: We also discovered that waste that's collected from households is taken to places called bring banks for sorting and baling into loads, one problem here is taking out everything that shouldn't have been placed in the recycling containers.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: People put all sorts of things into bottle banks, like plastic bags and even broken umbrellas.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: All this has to be removed by hand.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Another difficulty is that toughened glass used for cooking doesn't fully melt at the temperature required for other glass.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: And so that also has to be picked out by hand.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Glass is easy to recycle because it can be reused over and over again without becoming weaker.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: 2 million tons of glass is thrown away each year.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: That is 7 billion bottles and jars, but only 500,000 tons of that is collected and recycled.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Oddly enough, half the glass that's collected is green, and a lot of that is imported.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: So more green glass is recycled than the UK needs, as a result.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: New uses are being developed for recycled glass, particularly green glass, for example, in fiberglass manufacture and water filtration.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: A company called CLF Aggregates makes a product for roads, and 30% of the material is crushed glass.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: For recycling paper, Britain comes second in Europe with 40 percent, behind Germany's amazing 70 percent.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: When recycling started, there were quality problems, so it was difficult to use recycled paper in office printers, but these problems have now been solved.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: And Martin's, based in South London, produces a range of office stationery which is 100% recycled, costs the same as normal paper.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: And is of equally high quality, but this high quality comes at a cost in terms of the waste produced during the process.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Over a third of the waste paper that comes in can't be used in the recycled paper, leaving the question of what to do with it.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: One firm, Papersave, currently sells this to farmers as a soil conditioner, though this practice will soon be banned because of transport costs and the smell.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: And the company is looking into the possibility of alternative uses.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Plastic causes problems, because there are so many different types of plastic in use today.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: And each one has to be dealt with differently.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Pacrite recycles all sorts of things, from bottles to car bumpers, and one of its most successful activities is recycling plastic bottles to make containers which are used all over the country to collect waste.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: The Save-a-Cup scheme was set up by the vending and plastics industries to recycle as many as possible of the three-and-a-half billion polystyrene cups used each year.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: At the moment, 500 million polycups are collected, processed, and sold on to other businesses such as Waterford, which turns the cups into pencils.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: And Johnson and Jones, a Welsh based firm, which has developed a wide variety of items, including business cards.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Well, to sum up, there seems to be plenty of research going on into how to reuse materials, but the biggest problem is getting people to think about recycling.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Instead of throwing things away, at least doing the research made us much more careful.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER

items: By 2008, carbon dioxide emissions need to be <input> lower than in 1990.
Recycling saves energy and reduces emissions from landfill sites and <input>.
People say that one problem is a lack of '<input>' sites for household waste. At the 'bring banks', household waste is sorted and unsuitable items removed.
Glass designed to be utilised for <input> cannot be recycled with other types of glass.
In the UK, <input> tons of glass is recycled each year.
body.title: HOUSEHOLD WASTE RECYCLING
